1: A type and introduction: 'The word of the LORD that came to Joel son of Pethuel.' Joel's prophetic vocation — receiving and transmitting the divine word — is a type of the incarnate Word. Every OT prophet who spoke 'the word of the LORD' was an imperfect vessel for the communication that would be made perfectly in Christ (Hebrews 1:1–2: in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son). Joel's word is notable for its double horizon: the present locust crisis and the eschatological Day of the LORD are linked, just as Christ's first advent and his parousia are linked in the NT's 'already and not yet' structure.
2: A shadow: 'Has anything like this ever happened in your days?' Joel's call to the elders frames the locust plague as an unprecedented event that demands intergenerational memory. The 'unprecedented' character of Joel's plague prefigures the unprecedented character of the Day of the LORD — and of the cross itself. At Calvary, the disciples asked whether anything like this had ever happened: the Son of God crucified, darkness over the land, the temple veil torn. The answer in both testaments: no, nothing like this has ever happened. The uniqueness of Joel's crisis points forward to the unique, unrepeatable event of the incarnation and atonement.
3: A type: 'Tell your children about it, and let your children tell their children, and their children to the next generation.' The intergenerational proclamation mandate mirrors the Passover command (Exodus 12:26–27: when your children ask, 'What does this mean?' you shall say ...) and the Great Commission (Matthew 28:19–20: go and make disciples of all nations ... teaching them to observe everything I have commanded). The locust event must be told and retold; likewise the death and resurrection of Christ are to be proclaimed until he comes (1 Corinthians 11:26: as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes).
4: A shadow: the four locusts — cutting, swarming, hopping, destroying — represent the exhaustive, comprehensive nature of the judgment that precedes the messianic restoration. Revelation 9:3–11 draws directly on Joel's locust imagery for the fifth-trumpet plague: locusts from the abyss, with power like scorpions, tormenting those without God's seal. The fourfold progression signals totality: nothing is left untouched. Christ's cross is the ultimate exhaustion of judgment — he bore the full weight of the covenant curse (Galatians 3:13: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us) so that nothing remains for those who are in him.
5: A type: 'Wake up, you drunkards, and weep!' Joel's call to the wine-benumbed to wake from stupor and mourn uses the sleeping/waking vocabulary that Paul applies to eschatological alertness: 1 Thessalonians 5:6–8 (let us not sleep as others do, but let us keep awake and be sober). The drunkards who do not see the locust crisis coming are the type of those who do not see the Day of the LORD approaching. Christ's parable of the sleeping virgins (Matthew 25:5–13) uses the same contrast: those who sleep are unprepared for the Bridegroom's coming. The call to wake and weep is the precondition for the joy that follows in Joel 2:21.
6: A shadow: 'A nation has invaded my land — powerful and beyond counting; its teeth are like lions' teeth, and it has the fangs of a lioness.' Revelation 9:8 borrows directly: the locust-army from the abyss has teeth like lions' teeth. The locust nation that invades YHWH's land is the type of the demonic forces that assault the covenant people — and ultimately of the spiritual powers of darkness that Christ defeated at the cross. Colossians 2:15: he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him. The devouring nation is met by a greater conqueror.
7: A type: 'It has stripped my vine bare and splintered my fig tree.' The vine and the fig tree are Israel's primary covenant symbols (Micah 4:4; John 1:48). The stripped vine is the judgment that precedes the Vine's true revelation: Christ as the true vine (John 15:1–5: I am the true vine, and my Father is the vinedresser ... every branch that does not bear fruit he takes away). The cursing of the fig tree (Matthew 21:19–20) and the cleansing of the temple are Jesus's enacted judgment on the stripped, fruitless institutions of the old covenant — the same judgment Joel describes, now carried out by the Lord of the vineyard himself.
8: A type: 'Mourn like a young woman dressed in sackcloth, grieving for the husband of her youth.' The widow mourning her young husband is the most acute image of irreversible loss in the OT. The bride-and-bridegroom vocabulary runs through the prophets (Hosea 2:19–20; Isaiah 54:5–6) and culminates in the NT (John 3:29: the friend of the bridegroom rejoices greatly at the bridegroom's voice; Ephesians 5:25–27; Revelation 19:7). The mourning of v8 is the church's experience in Christ's absence — the disciples mourned like those who had lost the bridegroom (Matthew 9:15: can the wedding guests mourn while the bridegroom is with them?). The mourning precedes and guarantees the wedding supper.
9: A shadow: 'The grain offering and drink offering are cut off from the house of the LORD.' The cessation of the daily liturgy is the most grievous effect of the locust plague: worship is interrupted. This foreshadows the ultimate interruption — the destruction of the temple in 70 CE — and its theological resolution in Christ: the offering that was cut off is replaced by the one perfect offering. Hebrews 10:14: by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified. The grain offering and drink offering — bread and wine — find their deepest fulfillment in the Eucharist, where Christ gives himself as the permanent liturgy that no locust can interrupt.
10: A shadow: 'The fields are ruined; the soil is in mourning. The grain is destroyed, the new wine dries up, the olive oil fails.' The triple failure of grain, wine, and oil mirrors the covenant curse structure of Deuteronomy 28:38–42 (you shall plant a vineyard but not harvest it ... the locust shall devour all your trees). More profoundly, the ruined land is the type of the creation subjected to futility (Romans 8:20–22: the whole creation has been groaning in the pains of childbirth until now). The restoration of grain and wine and oil (Joel 2:19, 24) is the eschatological promise that Christ's redemption will reclaim all of creation, not merely individual souls.
11: A type: 'Be ashamed, O farmers; wail, O vinedressers — because the harvest of the field has perished.' The shame of those who worked but did not harvest is the type of the eschatological harvest failure for those who are not 'in the vine' (John 15:6: if anyone does not abide in me he is thrown away like a branch and withers). Conversely, those who abide in Christ bear fruit at the final harvest (Matthew 13:30: gather the wheat into my barn). The shame of Joel's farmers who tended the crop but lost the harvest is reversed in the restored harvest of Joel 2:24, grounded in the union with Christ who is himself the grain of wheat that fell into the earth and died (John 12:24).
12: A shadow: 'The vine has dried up and the fig tree withers — the pomegranate, the palm, and the apple tree — all the trees of the field are dried up, and joy has dried up from the children of man.' All seven trees and all joy are gone. The comprehensiveness of this desolation is the type of the spiritual barrenness that Christ confronts at his coming (Luke 18:8: when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on earth?). But the withered vine and fig — the very symbols of Israel's covenant blessing — find their resurrection in Christ: he is the vine that does not wither (John 15:1), and those who are in him will never go thirsty (John 4:14).
13: A type: 'Put on sackcloth and lament, O priests; wail, O ministers of the altar. Come, spend the night in sackcloth, O ministers of my God.' The priests weeping at the altar is the OT's model of intercessory grief on behalf of the people. Hebrews 7:25: Christ lives to make intercession for the people — but his intercession is not one of sackcloth and weeping but of triumphant high priestly presentation of his own blood (Hebrews 9:12: he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption). Joel's weeping priests point forward to the one High Priest whose intercession never needs to be renewed.
14: A type: 'Consecrate a fast; call a solemn assembly. Gather the elders and all the inhabitants of the land to the house of the LORD your God, and cry out to the LORD.' The solemn assembly before YHWH in the face of catastrophe is the OT liturgical pattern that Christ fulfills in his gathering of all peoples to the Father. Matthew 18:20: where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them. The fast that Joel commands is answered by Christ, who taught his disciples that prayer and fasting were the appropriate response to the powers that cannot be overcome otherwise (Mark 9:29). The assembly before the house of YHWH points to the new temple that Christ's body is (John 2:21).
15: A direct revelation: 'Alas for the day! For the day of the LORD is near, and it comes as devastation from the Almighty.' The Day of the LORD is the single most prominent eschatological theme in Joel — and in the NT, it becomes the Day of Christ. 1 Thessalonians 5:2: the day of the Lord comes like a thief in the night. 2 Peter 3:10: the day of the Lord will come like a thief, and then the heavens will pass away. The Almighty who brings the Day in Joel is identified in Revelation as the Alpha and Omega (Revelation 1:8: who is and who was and who is to come, the Almighty — pantokrator). Christ is the one through whom the Day of the LORD is both announced and administered.
16: A shadow: 'Is not the food already cut off before our eyes, and joy and gladness from the house of our God?' The disappearance of food and joy from the house of YHWH is the inverse of the new covenant's eucharistic abundance. John 6:35: I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst. The joy that was cut off from the house of God is restored in the Spirit (Galatians 5:22: the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace ...), and the eucharistic feast is the anticipation of the wedding supper where abundance cannot be cut off (Revelation 19:9).
17: A shadow: 'The seeds have shrivelled in the ground; the storehouses stand empty; the granaries are torn down, for the grain has dried up.' The dead seed in the ground is the precondition for the harvest of resurrection. John 12:24: unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit. Christ is the grain that fell into the earth — the death that appeared to be the final shriveling was actually the beginning of the harvest. The empty storehouses of Joel's crisis are transformed into the full threshing floors of 2:24 through the death and resurrection of the one true Seed.
18: A shadow: 'How the animals groan! The herds of cattle are confused because there is no pasture for them; even the flocks of sheep suffer.' The groaning of animals in Joel's crisis is the specific sound of what Paul describes in cosmic terms: Romans 8:22 — the whole creation has been groaning together in the pains of childbirth until now. The animals who cannot find pasture are the type of all creation waiting for the liberation that comes with the revelation of the sons of God (Romans 8:19–21). Christ's restoration of creation — beginning with the resurrection, consummated in the new creation — is the answer to the groan of Joel's cattle.
19: A type: 'To you, O LORD, I cry out. For fire has devoured the open pastures and flame has burned all the trees of the field.' The prophet's prayer in the midst of consuming fire is the type of the intercession that the Spirit makes through the saints (Romans 8:26: the Spirit helps us in our weakness ... the Spirit himself intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words). The fire that devours the pastures is the covenant curse; the prayer that rises from that fire is the covenant appeal. Christ from the cross cried out (Matthew 27:46: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?) — the deepest cry from within the consuming fire of divine wrath, heard and answered in the resurrection.
20: A shadow: 'Even the wild animals pant and cry to you, for the water brooks have dried up and fire has devoured the open pastures.' The panting of wild animals for dried-up water echoes Psalms 42:1 (as a deer pants for flowing streams, so pants my soul for you, O God). The dried water brooks of Joel's crisis point to the Living Water that Christ offers: John 7:37–38 (if anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink; out of his heart will flow rivers of living water — speaking of the Spirit). The panting of every creature for water in Joel 1 is answered in Joel 2's dew and rain (Joel 2:23) and fulfilled permanently in Revelation 22:17 (the Spirit and the Bride say Come; let the one who is thirsty come).
